column1: Apollonius of Tyana’s life, with historical sources, parable-like episodes, and documentation of attributed parables. All sources are cited in standard academic form.

column3: Summary/Parable-like Elements
Born in Tyana, Cappadocia; miraculous portents at birth (e.g., swans singing, lightning).
column6: Parallels to miraculous births in ancient literature (e.g., Pythagoras, Jesus).

column3: At age 16, adopts Pythagorean practices: vegetarianism, celibacy, silence, giving away inheritance.
column5: “As the wise man is a temple of God, so too is the body a temple of the soul.”
column6: Emphasis on self-mastery and discipline; echoes of Pythagorean and Stoic parables.

column3: Lives at Aegae, gains fame for healing and prophecy.
column5: “The wise man should resemble the physician, whose first duty is to heal himself.”
column6: Miraculous healings; parable of the physician.

column3: Five years of silence; travels through Asia Minor, calming disputes.
column5: “Silence is the safest response for all the contradiction that arises from ignorance.”
column6: Parable-like episodes of resolving conflict through non-action and wisdom.

column3: Travels to Babylon, India, Egypt; learns from Brahmans, Magi, Egyptian priests.
column5: “The gods do not need sacrifices, but a pure mind.”
column6: Parable of the Indian sages: wisdom is found in simplicity and virtue, not ritual.

column3: Returns to Greece, performs miracles (e.g., revives a Roman bride, exorcises demons).
column5: “The soul is imprisoned by the passions, as a bird by a cage.”
column6: Resurrection of the bride: parallels to Gospel miracles; interpreted as a parable of spiritual awakening.

column3: Advises Vespasian on just rulership; predicts his rise to emperor.
column4: Philostratus, VA 5.27–5.41; Cassius Dio, Roman History 66.12
column5: “A good ruler is like a gardener: he must prune, not destroy.”
column6: Parable of the gardener; Philostratus dramatizes political influence.

column3: Performs miracles, heals, exorcises, foretells events (e.g., plague, Domitian’s death).
column5: “As the sun shines on both the just and the unjust, so should the wise man’s charity.”
column6: Parable of the sun; used by pagans as counter-narrative to Christian miracles.

column3: Accused of sorcery and treason; allegedly vanishes from court or is acquitted.
column5: “Injustice is like a shadow: it flees before the light of truth.”
column6: Parable of the shadow; variations in accounts of trial outcome.

column3: Dies or vanishes; followers claim ascension to heaven.
column5: “The soul’s journey is not ended by death, but by ignorance.”
column6: Apotheosis motif; shrines and cultic veneration.

column3: Emperor Aurelian spares Tyana after vision of Apollonius.
Letters attributed to Apollonius circulate; revered as sage and healer.
column4: Epistolae Apollonii; Arabic Book of Balinas
column5: “Wisdom is the only wealth that cannot be stolen.”
column6: Many letters are considered spurious or pseudepigraphal.

column3: Eusebius compares Apollonius to Jesus, challenges miracle accounts.
column6: Apollonius used as pagan counterpoint to Christ in polemics.

column1: Here is a more complete table of parables and parable-like teachings attributed to Apollonius of Tyana, based on your list and expanded with additional relevant material from primary sources and scholarly summaries:

column3: Quotation / Description
“The wise man should resemble the physician, whose first duty is to heal himself.”
“A good ruler is like a gardener: he must prune, not destroy.”
“As the sun shines on both the just and the unjust, so should the wise man’s charity.”
“Injustice is like a shadow: it flees before the light of truth.”
“The soul is imprisoned by the passions, as a bird by a cage.”
“The soul’s journey is not ended by death, but by ignorance.”
“The gods do not need sacrifices, but a pure mind.”
Apollonius revives a Roman bride, interpreted as a sign of spiritual awakening and purity.
Apollonius learns from Indian sages that virtue and wisdom are higher than ritual and material wealth.
His vanishing at Domitian’s court is presented as a parable of the triumph of innocence over tyranny.
A spring brings justice to perjurers and refreshment to the virtuous, symbolizing the impartiality of divine justice.
Apollonius gives his inheritance to his wayward brother, saying, “I will heal him of these bad passions,” showing virtue over kinship.
“The gods love men of virtue and welcome them without any instructions.”
Prefers wild herbs to artificial garden produce, teaching natural simplicity over luxury.

column1: Parable of the Vow of Silence
column3: Only those who have kept a four-year vow of silence may learn his secret rites, symbolizing discipline and spiritual readiness.
“Everything is worn and withered away by time, whereas time itself never ages, but remains immortal because of memory.”
“Because I asked questions when I was a lad; and it is not my business to ask questions now, but to teach people what I have discovered.”
Apollonius praises Aesop: “Aesop, who wrote all those fables, always included a moral truth to his short stories or parables.”

column1: This table includes both direct parables and parable-like sayings or episodes, reflecting Apollonius’s style of teaching through metaphor, moral example, and symbolic action. The references to Philostratus’s Life of Apollonius of Tyana (VA) are the principal ancient source for these teachings, supplemented by summaries and interpretations from modern and secondary sources238.
